name: Under Armour, Inc.

profile.description: Under Armour, Inc. designs, develops, markets, and distributes performance apparel, footwear, and accessories for men, women, and youth. The company operates under brands including Under Armour, HeatGear, ColdGear, HOVR, and Armour Fleece. Products are sold through wholesale channels (sporting goods chains, department stores, specialty retailers) and direct-to-consumer via owned retail stores and e-commerce. Founded in 1996 and headquartered in Baltimore, Maryland, the company operates across North America, EMEA, Asia-Pacific, and Latin America.

innovation.label: WEAK
innovation.analysis: Track Record (1/3): Under Armour has not executed a meaningful product or business model transformation in the past five years. The company lost its way chasing lifestyle apparel and international expansion, then under-invested in premium performance product. CEO Kevin Plank's return in 2024 initiated a turnaround — cutting 25% of SKUs, eliminating deep discounts, and pivoting back to sport performance — but this is remediation, not reinvention. One modest point awarded for the NEOLAST sustainable material commercialization and the re-commitment to performance roots [1]. AI Deployment (0/2): No AI-powered consumer products or quantified AI-driven cost savings were identified. The company has not disclosed any material AI deployment strategy in public filings or earnings calls [2]. Market Share (0/2): Under Armour is actively losing market share to Nike, Adidas, lululemon, and New Balance in its core performance apparel and footwear categories. Revenue has declined from $5.9B in FY2023 to $5.0B in FY2026, a 16% contraction over three years [1]. Product Pipeline (1/2): The Fall/Winter 2025 collection (fiscal H2 FY2026) has been specifically flagged as the first major rollout of elevated premium product, with management citing this as the inflection point for brand repositioning [1][3]. R&D Intensity (0/1): As a performance apparel company, formal R&D spend is not separately disclosed and is not believed to be above industry average; no evidence of top-quartile innovation investment relative to sector peers.

inspiration.label: WEAK
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (2/4): Under Armour's mission — 'to make all athletes better through passion, design, and the relentless pursuit of innovation' — is clear and identifies who the company serves, but it does not articulate a deeper purpose that differentiates it from any other athletic apparel brand. The 'relentless pursuit of innovation' claim rings hollow given the company's recent execution failures [4]. Community Impact (1/2): Under Armour's Project Rampart initiative supports student-athletes in Baltimore schools, and the annual 'Armour Day' in September 2025 deployed over 1,000 teammates as volunteers across 13 locations. The company committed to impacting 100,000 youth and renovating 20 safe places to play — genuine community investment anchored in their hometown [4]. Employee Retention (0/2): The company is mid-restructuring with $305M in restructuring charges planned through FY2027 and significant headcount reductions; no evidence of above-average retention or employee tenure data was found. The revolving-door CEO dynamic (Plank founded company, left, returned) signals organizational instability [2]. Insider Buying (0/2): No meaningful net insider buying by CEO or executives was identified in the last 12 months. Kevin Plank holds founder-level equity through Class C shares but has not been reported making open-market purchases.

infrastructure.label: DEVELOPING
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored as a Growth stock. Under Armour's financials reflect a company in deep restructuring. Revenue declined -0.8% YoY in the most recent period and has contracted ~3% annually over 5 years from the CY2021 base. Net losses of -$496M in FY2026 and -$201M in FY2025 drove ROE deeply negative (-30%) and profit margins to -10%, both scoring zero. Debt-to-equity of 137% is elevated but scores partially (0.71) given headroom before the 300% floor. Forward P/E of 23.9 scores well (0.90) on the basis of analyst expectations for a return to profitability. Forward PEG of 1.84 reflects recovery optionality at partial credit (0.58). The liquidity check passes easily at ~$53M average daily dollar volume. Scores drag significantly on the double-weighted ROE, revenue CAGR, and PEG metrics.

ai_resilience.label: MODERATE
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (7/9): Under Armour's core business — designing, manufacturing, and selling physical performance apparel and footwear — is structurally resilient to AI displacement. No AI tool can replace the physical products themselves, the global supply chain required to produce them, or the wholesale and direct-to-consumer retail relationships the company depends on. Performance apparel has physical requirements (compression, thermal management, moisture wicking) that cannot be digitally substituted. Analysts have not flagged AI as a material risk to the apparel manufacturing industry's core economics [2]. However, AI does create real pressure on adjacent functions: design automation could compress product development cycles (benefiting or threatening UA's teams), AI-driven personalization favors larger-data-platform competitors like Nike, and automated digital marketing threatens to widen the gap between UA and better-funded rivals. These are operational risks, not existential ones, placing the company in the PROTECTED range. AI Opportunity (0/1): Under Armour has not disclosed any meaningful revenue generation from AI-related products or services. No specific AI revenue figures were identified [2].

infinite_game.summary: Under Armour scores low on Infinite Game because its history reflects reactive, short-term decision-making far more than principled long-term stewardship. The company abandoned its performance-sport Just Cause in pursuit of lifestyle market share, then reversed course only when financial pressure forced it. Leadership instability — Plank's departure, a succession of CEOs, and his return — signals an organization reacting to quarterly results rather than advancing an enduring cause. Sinek would see a company that has had a Just Cause but lacked the courage and organizational consistency to live by it.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (1/3): Under Armour's stated cause — making all athletes better — has genuine depth and is specific to a community of people. But it has not been validated by consistent execution. Between 2016 and 2022, the company pivoted toward lifestyle apparel and celebrity partnerships that had little to do with making athletes better, diluting its performance identity and ceding share to Nike and lululemon. The Just Cause exists as a statement, but strategic decisions have repeatedly undermined it. Plank's return and the current 'back to sport' pivot may signal recommitment, but it has not yet been proven through a multi-year period of consistent execution [1][3].

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (0/2): The evidence points to a low-trust organizational environment. Under Armour has executed multiple rounds of restructuring ($305M total planned charges), cycling through multiple CEOs and leadership teams in under a decade. The founder's return after being ousted signals that organizational continuity broke down. Sinek would identify the repeated leadership disruption and ongoing cost-cutting as hallmarks of an organization where people spend energy protecting themselves rather than advancing the cause [1][2].

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (0/1): Under Armour's competitive posture has historically been about 'protecting this house' against Nike — a deeply finite framing. Management commentary tends to reference winning categories and regaining market share rather than acknowledging what rivals do better and using that as fuel for improvement. The turnaround narrative is reactive to competitive losses rather than proactively inspired by competitors' strengths.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (1/2): Under Armour has demonstrated some strategic adaptation — expanding from compression apparel into footwear, connected fitness (MapMyFitness acquisition), and international markets. The company also divested its connected fitness apps in 2021. These are incremental pivots rather than transformative reinvention. The current 'back to core sport performance' restructuring could prove to be meaningful strategic flexibility, but it reads more as returning to roots under financial pressure than as a proactive, cause-driven reinvention [1][3].

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (1/2): Kevin Plank's return and the decision to absorb $305M in restructuring charges while sacrificing near-term revenue to eliminate discount promotions and cut 25% of SKUs represents genuine short-term pain for long-term positioning [1]. The willingness to shrink revenue in service of brand integrity earns partial credit. However, this also reads as correcting self-inflicted wounds rather than the kind of proactive, principle-driven courage Sinek describes — like NVDA investing in CUDA a decade before the AI boom.

investment_thesis: Under Armour is a turnaround story at a crossroads. CEO Kevin Plank returned in 2024 with a clear mandate: cut the bloat (25% SKU reduction), eliminate promotional discounting, and recommit to performance sport where the brand was built. The Fall/Winter 2025 collection (fiscal H2 FY2026) represents the first tangible test of the repositioned product strategy. At $5.78 per share and ~$2.5B market cap, UAA trades at a significant discount to its intrinsic value if the turnaround succeeds — the stock once traded above $50 per share and the brand has global awareness, wholesale relationships, and a loyal core athletic consumer. The balance sheet has room to execute with $309M in cash and $591M in long-term debt, though $305M in planned restructuring charges will be a continued drag through FY2027 [1][2].
thesis_continued: The bear case is that Under Armour's window to recapture meaningful market share may have permanently closed. Revenue has declined from $5.9B to under $5.0B in three years while Nike, lululemon, New Balance, and On Running have gained the premium sport and lifestyle ground that UA abandoned. The brand's identity is muddled — neither the clear performance leader nor a compelling lifestyle brand — and rebuilding brand equity is a multi-year, capital-intensive process. With negative ROE (-30%), negative profit margins (-10%), and a history of execution misses against turnaround timelines, the risk of continued value destruction is real. Tariff headwinds (manufacturing concentrated in Asia) added further pressure in Q4 FY2026, with gross margin contracting 470 bps to 42% [1][3].
key_catalysts: Fall/Winter 2025 product launch (H2 FY2026) delivers elevated premium performance apparel at higher ASPs, demonstrating the turnaround's first proof point and stemming the revenue decline
Return to GAAP profitability in FY2027 as restructuring charges roll off, discounting is eliminated, and SG&A normalization from $2.3B toward sustainable levels validates the margin recovery thesis

Kevin Plank successfully recaptures the 18-34 male performance athlete consumer with renewed marketing storytelling around sport authenticity, arresting market share losses to New Balance and On Running
key_risks: Revenue decline continues below $4.5B as wholesale partners reduce shelf space allocations in response to UA's brand weakness, while DTC traffic fails to offset wholesale channel losses
Tariff exposure on Asian-manufactured goods (estimated 15-25% of COGS) compounds the margin recovery challenge, making the path to double-digit operating margins structurally harder

Kevin Plank's founder-centric leadership style creates key-man risk and organizational instability; if the brand repositioning fails to resonate by mid-FY2027, the company may face pressure to explore strategic alternatives at distressed valuations
